flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in a balanced BST?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - because we eliminate half of the remaining nodes with each comparison, similar to binary search.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity for search in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(n) - occurs when the BST is completely skewed (like a linked list), where we must traverse all nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of insertion in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(h) where h is the height of the tree. Best case O(log n), worst case O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of deletion in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(h) where h is the height. Requires finding the node, potentially finding replacement (in-order successor/predecessor), and restructuring.

flashcards.front: What is the height of a balanced BST with n nodes?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - a balanced BST like AVL maintains height approximately log₂(n+1).

flashcards.front: What is the height of a completely skewed BST with n nodes?
flashcards.back: O(n) - the tree degenerates into a linear structure like a linked list.
For each node, all nodes in the left subtree have keys less than the node's key, and all nodes in the right subtree have keys greater than the node's key.

flashcards.front: What is the average-case time complexity for BST operations?
flashcards.back: O(log n) for a randomly built BST, as the expected height is approximately 4.3 log₁₀(n).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of finding the minimum element in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(h) - traverse left children until reaching the leftmost node. Best case O(1) if minimum is root.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of finding the maximum element in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(h) - traverse right children until reaching the rightmost node. Best case O(1) if maximum is root.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of in-order traversal in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(n) - must visit all n nodes regardless of tree balance. This gives sorted output.

flashcards.front: Why is BST better than linear search for large datasets?
flashcards.back: BST provides O(log n) average search time vs O(n) for linear search, enabling much faster lookups as data grows.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between BST height and time complexity?
flashcards.back: All BST operations (search, insert, delete) have time complexity O(h), where h is the tree height. Balancing ensures h = O(log n).
A BST that automatically maintains logarithmic height through rotations or rebalancing after insertions/deletions (e.g., AVL, Red-Black trees).
